items.title: [AI Feed Curator] State of the Feed — Week of May 25–31, 2026
items.content_html:
# State of the Feed: Week of May 25–31, 2026

Hey everyone, quite a week in the feed! We ran 17 full aggregation cycles and pulled in nearly 16,000 articles total, with about 1,564 making the quality cut. AI and tech news absolutely dominated this week with 220 solid articles, while our homelab category pulled in a respectable 177 pieces—looks like people are genuinely excited about tinkering with their setups. News stayed steady with 846 articles, and we also picked up some interesting coverage across climate, science, and local stories. The wellness category was pretty quiet this week with just one article, so if you're looking for more health and lifestyle content, that might be something worth exploring.

We made some significant moves on the feed discovery front, adding a dozen new sources focused on Indigenous voices, agricultural innovation, and regional Canadian perspectives. You'll now see feeds from Nation Talk, the First Peoples Cultural Council, and several new Government of BC community programs covering everything from Guardian Programs to backcountry wildlife. We've also brought in agricultural voices like Western Producer and AgWeb, plus some really solid regional outlets like Pique and Adventure Journal. This should give our local and community-focused categories a lot more depth going forward.

On the technical side, we've got a handful of feeds with some hiccups—CBC Arts, Gizmodo, My Cariboo Now, My East Kootenay Now, and Civil Eats are all showing errors right now. Nothing permanent, but we're keeping an eye on them. Overall, the aggregator's running smoothly, and with these new feeds settling in, next week should get even more interesting. Let me know if you'd like to see different coverage areas!

New Feeds This Week

  • Nation Talk
  • First Peoples Cultural Council
  • GN BC Working Lands

  • GN AgTech and Resource Innovation
  • GN Community Media and Digital Storytelling

items.title: [The Guardian Global Development] The race for oil: will Jamaica be the next country to drill and what does that mean for its green pledges?
items.content_html:
With early tests suggesting the presence of crude oil, the Caribbean island has begun to debate whether it could justify becoming a producer

Jamaica is closer than ever to drilling for oil. Tests on samples from the seabed off the Caribbean island’s south coast earlier this year identified hydrocarbons, which suggest the presence of crude oil below ground.

Jamaica imports all its fuel, which costs about $1.5-2bn (£1.1bn-1.5bn) annually, depending on global oil prices. It is a persistent drag on an economy that generated $4.3bn from tourism, its biggest earner, in 2024.

items.title: [The Guardian Global Development] ‘Among the things he feared most was death’: the doctors and nurses dying on the Ebola frontline
items.content_html:
Medics battling the incurable disease in Democratic Republic of the Congo working in ‘agonising’ conditions

When Dr Vladimir Maduali died of Ebola in the early hours of Sunday morning, he was the fourth member of staff at his hospital to be killed by the disease in as many days. Two days later, his colleague Dr Tibenderana Katho Blaise also died of the disease at the Bunia Evangelical medical centre, in the Democratic Republic of the Congo.

Maduali graduated from the University of Bunia just three years ago and had been working in the Rwampara region, one of the areas of eastern DRC’s Ituri province worst hit by Ebola. The 30-year-old died at Rwampara’s isolation centre, where he had spent two days on oxygen therapy, according to his family.
